summary: France's premier sparkling wine region, northeast of Paris, producing wines by the traditional method (méthode champenoise) with secondary fermentation in bottle. The three principal grapes are Chardonnay (Blanc de Blancs), Pinot Noir and Pinot Meunier (together Blanc de Noirs), assembled across vintages into non-vintage cuvées or bottled as vintage and prestige cuvées.
classification: Champagne uses the Échelle des Crus ('ladder of growth'), a village-rating system from 80% to 100% established in 1919. The 17 villages rated 100% are Grands Crus and the 42 villages rated 90-99% are Premiers Crus. A crucial distinction is between Maisons/Négociants (the large Grandes Marques houses, code NM, who buy grapes) and Récoltant-Manipulant 'Grower Champagnes' (code RM, who grow and vinify their own fruit).

producers.name: Krug

summary: A long, narrow region on the eastern flank of the Vosges Mountains along the German border, producing predominantly aromatic, varietally-labelled white wines. The signature grapes are the four 'noble' varieties Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris and Muscat, alongside Pinot Blanc, Sylvaner and Pinot Noir (the only red); Crémant d'Alsace is the traditional-method sparkling.
classification: Alsace has three AOCs: Alsace (regional, usually varietal), Crémant d'Alsace (sparkling), and Alsace Grand Cru, which covers 51 delimited single vineyards restricted mainly to the four noble grapes. Schlossberg was the first Grand Cru (1975); Kaefferkopf the most recent (2007); Rangen is the only volcanic site. Sweet wines are labelled Vendanges Tardives (late harvest) and Sélection de Grains Nobles (botrytised).

summary: France's longest wine region, stretching ~1,000 km along the Loire river from the Atlantic to the Massif Central, and the largest producer of white and rosé wines in the country. Its emblematic grapes are Chenin Blanc (dry to sweet and sparkling), Sauvignon Blanc (Sancerre, Pouilly-Fumé), Cabernet Franc (Chinon, Bourgueil, Saumur-Champigny) and Melon de Bourgogne (Muscadet).
classification: The Loire is organised by appellation rather than a vineyard cru hierarchy, with around 50 AOCs grouped in four sub-regions: Pays Nantais (Muscadet), Anjou-Saumur (Chenin Blanc and Cabernet Franc; sweet Coteaux du Layon and Quarts de Chaume Grand Cru, dry Savennières), Touraine (Vouvray and Montlouis Chenin; Chinon, Bourgueil Cabernet Franc) and Centre-Loire (Sancerre, Pouilly-Fumé, Menetou-Salon, Quincy, Reuilly). Quarts de Chaume is the Loire's sole Grand Cru.
